patient.chief_complaint: persistent anemia despite iron supplementation for 2+ years

records.summary: Annual physical â€” recurrent anemia evaluation
records.content: 38 y/o female with recurrent iron deficiency anemia (IDA) x 3 years. On ferrous sulfate 325mg BID. Also has hypothyroidism on levothyroxine, IBS with bloating/diarrhea on dicyclomine, and chronic fatigue. Reports ongoing fatigue despite iron supplementation and thyroid being 'controlled.' Menstrual history: regular periods, moderate flow â€” previously assumed as cause of IDA. Vitals: BP 110/68, HR 78, BMI 22.1, weight 128 lbs. Exam: pale conjunctivae, mild glossitis noted. Assessment: Persistent IDA despite adequate supplementation â€” need to investigate further. Plan: Labs including iron studies, CBC, thyroid. Consider GI evaluation if anemia not improving.

records.summary: Lab review â€” severe iron deficiency despite treatment
records.content: 38 y/o female. Ferritin 8 â€” critically low DESPITE being on ferrous sulfate 325mg BID for over 2 years. Not absorbing iron. Possible causes: 1) Non-compliance (patient insists she takes it), 2) Omeprazole reducing iron absorption (PPIs reduce gastric acid needed for iron), 3) Ongoing blood loss exceeding supplementation, 4) Malabsorption. Assessment: Refractory iron deficiency anemia. Plan: Verify compliance â€” switch to liquid iron (easier to verify). Ensure taking on empty stomach with vitamin C. Separate from omeprazole by 2 hours. GYN referral for heavy menstrual bleeding evaluation. If still not improving in 3 months, consider IV iron and GI evaluation.

38 y/o female referred for evaluation of heavy menstrual bleeding as possible cause of refractory IDA. Menstrual history: cycles q28 days, duration 5 days, uses 3-4 regular pads/day. Reports occasional cramping but no clots. Pelvic exam: normal. Pelvic ultrasound: normal uterus, no fibroids, no polyps, normal endometrial thickness. Assessment: Menstrual bleeding appears NORMAL â€” not heavy enough to explain severe refractory IDA. Plan: No GYN intervention needed. Suggest further workup for other causes of iron loss or malabsorption.

38 y/o female. GYN evaluation normal â€” menstrual bleeding is NOT the cause of refractory IDA. Still on iron + omeprazole. Assessment: If not menstrual loss and taking iron, then must be malabsorption or occult GI loss. Plan: Check stool guaiac. Consider GI referral. Also: omeprazole is reducing iron absorption â€” discuss stepping down.

38 y/o female. IBS symptoms: intermittent bloating, diarrhea alternating with normal stools, abdominal cramping. On dicyclomine PRN. Diet: no specific restrictions. Reports fatigue worsening. Assessment: IBS, chronic fatigue, persistent IDA. Plan: Trial of low-FODMAP diet for IBS. Continue iron. Fatigue likely from anemia.

38 y/o female. Low-FODMAP diet helped bloating somewhat. Still fatigued. Iron studies show minimal improvement. Assessment: Refractory IDA â€” 3 months of continued supplementation with little improvement. Plan: IV iron infusion (ferric carboxymaltose 750mg). Recheck labs in 4 weeks post-infusion.
38 y/o female. Ferric carboxymaltose 750mg IV infused over 15 min. No adverse reaction. Plan: Repeat in 1 week. Check labs 4 weeks post-second infusion.
38 y/o female. Second dose ferric carboxymaltose 750mg IV. Total dose: 1500mg. No reaction. Plan: Labs in 4 weeks.

38 y/o female. Excellent response to IV iron â€” ferritin from 8 to 120, hemoglobin 10.2 â†’ 11.8. Feeling somewhat better, more energy. Assessment: IDA responding to IV iron but NOT to oral iron â€” confirms malabsorption of oral iron. The fact that she responded to IV but not oral strongly suggests a GI absorption issue. Plus she's on omeprazole which reduces gastric acid (needed for iron absorption). Plan: Continue oral iron maintenance. Consider GI referral for malabsorption workup. Recheck in 3 months.

39 y/o female. Ferritin already dropped from 120 to 42 in 3 months. Hemoglobin declining again (11.8 â†’ 11.2). This is rapid depletion â€” either ongoing loss or continued malabsorption on oral iron. Assessment: Refractory IDA with rapid iron depletion. Oral iron not maintaining stores. Plan: GI referral for malabsorption workup. May need periodic IV iron infusions.

39 y/o female referred for refractory IDA. Iron stores rapidly deplete despite supplementation. Responds to IV but not oral iron. Stool guaiac negative. GYN workup normal. Also has IBS-like symptoms (bloating, diarrhea). On omeprazole 20mg daily. Assessment: Refractory IDA with malabsorption pattern. Differential: 1) PPI-related iron malabsorption, 2) Celiac disease, 3) H. pylori, 4) Occult GI pathology. Plan: EGD with duodenal biopsies (celiac evaluation). Celiac serologies. H. pylori testing. Colonoscopy (age-appropriate screening + rule out occult blood loss source).

39 y/o female. tTG-IgA: 98 (strongly positive â€” normal < 20). Total IgA normal (rules out IgA deficiency). This is HIGHLY suggestive of celiac disease. This explains EVERYTHING: 1) Refractory iron deficiency (malabsorption in proximal small bowel), 2) Failure of oral iron (can't absorb it), 3) Response to IV iron (bypasses gut), 4) Rapid depletion of iron stores (ongoing malabsorption), 5) IBS-like symptoms (bloating, diarrhea â€” classic celiac), 6) Chronic fatigue. Assessment: Celiac disease â€” serologically confirmed. Plan: Proceed with EGD + duodenal biopsy for histological confirmation (Marsh classification). Colonoscopy still planned. Begin education about gluten-free diet.

39 y/o female. EGD: Duodenum appears scalloped/flattened (loss of normal villous pattern). Biopsies taken from D1 and D2. Colonoscopy: Normal. No polyps, no inflammation, no bleeding source. Pathology results: Duodenal biopsies show villous atrophy (Marsh 3b), increased intraepithelial lymphocytes, crypt hyperplasia. Assessment: CELIAC DISEASE â€” histologically confirmed. Marsh 3b (subtotal villous atrophy). Plan: Strict gluten-free diet (GFD). Dietitian referral. Check additional nutritional labs (vitamin D, calcium, B12, folate, zinc). DEXA scan (celiac increases osteoporosis risk). Recheck tTG-IgA in 6-12 months to confirm dietary compliance. Continue iron supplementation. May be able to stop omeprazole after GFD as GERD symptoms may improve.

39 y/o female. Celiac disease confirmed. Multiple nutritional deficiencies: iron (chronic), vitamin D (18 â€” deficient), calcium (8.4 â€” low), zinc (55 â€” low), albumin (3.4 â€” low). Assessment: Celiac disease with malabsorption-related nutritional deficiencies. Plan: Strict gluten-free diet. Vitamin D 5000 IU daily. Calcium citrate 600mg BID (citrate â€” better absorbed without acid, unlike carbonate). Continue iron. Zinc supplementation 25mg daily. DEXA scan. Dietitian referral. Recheck labs in 3-6 months.

39 y/o female. Started gluten-free diet 4 weeks ago. Reports significant improvement in bloating and diarrhea. Less abdominal cramping. Dicyclomine use decreased. Energy slightly improved. Assessment: Early positive response to GFD. Plan: Continue GFD strictly. Labs in 2 months.

39 y/o female with celiac disease. DEXA: Lumbar spine T-score -1.8 (osteopenia). Hip T-score -1.2 (normal). Assessment: Osteopenia â€” likely from celiac-related calcium and vitamin D malabsorption. Plan: Continue vitamin D 5000 IU + calcium citrate. Repeat DEXA in 2 years. GFD should help absorption improve.

39 y/o female. 3 months on GFD. All labs improving. tTG-IgA declining (98 â†’ 45). Iron stores rebuilding on oral iron now (ferritin 35 â€” previously couldn't get above 10 on oral). Vitamin D improving. Calcium normalized. Bloating and diarrhea 80% improved. Assessment: Celiac disease responding well to GFD. Iron absorption improving. Plan: Continue strict GFD. Continue supplements. Next labs in 6 months.

39 y/o female. Dramatic improvement since celiac diagnosis. GFD well-maintained. Energy much better. GI symptoms minimal. Weight 132 lbs (+4 lbs â€” healthy gain). Assessment: Celiac disease well-managed. Plan: Labs.

hemoglobin.flag: NORMAL â€” FIRST TIME IN YEARS

records.content: 39 y/o female. Hemoglobin 12.4 â€” NORMAL for the first time in years! Ferritin 58 â€” healthy stores. tTG-IgA negative â€” confirmed GFD compliance and mucosal healing. Vitamin D sufficient. Calcium normal. Assessment: Celiac disease in remission on GFD. Iron deficiency RESOLVED. Plan: Continue GFD lifelong. Continue vitamin D + calcium. Can likely stop iron supplementation â€” recheck ferritin in 6 months to confirm stores maintained.

40 y/o female. Celiac in remission. Off iron supplementation x 4 months (trial). Feeling great. GFD maintained. IBS symptoms essentially resolved. Assessment: Excellent. Plan: Labs to confirm iron stores maintained.

40 y/o female. Ferritin 52, hemoglobin 12.6 â€” iron stores maintained without supplementation! This confirms the GFD has restored intestinal absorption. Assessment: Celiac disease in full remission. Iron deficiency resolved. Plan: No iron supplementation needed. Continue GFD, vitamin D, calcium. Annual labs.

40 y/o female. On omeprazole 20mg daily for 'GERD' x 4 years. Now that celiac is diagnosed and treated, GI symptoms have resolved. GERD symptoms: patient reports no heartburn in over 6 months. Assessment: GERD symptoms resolved â€” likely were celiac-related all along. Plan: Trial PPI discontinuation: omeprazole 20mg every other day x 2 weeks, then stop. Switch to famotidine 20mg PRN if any breakthrough reflux.

40 y/o female. Omeprazole stopped x 2 weeks. No GERD symptoms. Assessment: GERD was likely celiac-related. Omeprazole no longer needed. This removes the PPI contribution to iron malabsorption (which was compounding the celiac malabsorption). Plan: Continue without PPI. Famotidine PRN if needed.

40 y/o female. Best health in years. Celiac in remission. No IDA. No GERD. IBS symptoms resolved (was celiac all along). Energy normalized. Weight 134 lbs. Off iron, off omeprazole, off dicyclomine. Current meds: levothyroxine 75mcg, vitamin D 2000 IU, calcium citrate 600mg. Assessment: Dramatic improvement with celiac diagnosis and treatment. Plan: Annual labs.
41 y/o female. Doing great. GFD maintained. No complaints. Assessment: Stable. Plan: Continue current simple regimen.

41 y/o female. On levothyroxine 75mcg daily. TSH stable. Note: Celiac disease and hypothyroidism are both autoimmune â€” association is well-documented. Patient should be aware of other autoimmune disease risk. Plan: Continue levothyroxine. Annual TSH.

41 y/o female with itchy, blistering rash on elbows and knees x 1 week. Symmetrical. Very pruritic. Exam: grouped vesicles on an erythematous base, bilateral elbows and knees. Assessment: Dermatitis herpetiformis â€” the skin manifestation of celiac disease. Despite GFD compliance (tTG normal), this can occur with even trace gluten exposure. Plan: Dermatology referral. May need dapsone for symptom control. Reinforce strict GFD â€” check for hidden gluten sources.

41 y/o female with celiac disease. Classic DH presentation â€” grouped vesicles on elbows and knees. Skin biopsy performed: direct immunofluorescence shows granular IgA deposits at dermal papillae â€” CONFIRMS dermatitis herpetiformis. Assessment: DH confirmed. Plan: Dapsone 50mg daily (first-line for DH). Check G6PD before starting (dapsone causes hemolytic anemia in G6PD deficiency). CBC monitoring. Reinforce strict GFD â€” with sustained GFD compliance, DH often improves and dapsone can be tapered.

41 y/o female. Dapsone 50mg daily x 4 weeks. Rash dramatically improved â€” vesicles resolved, mild residual erythema. No hemolysis on labs. Assessment: DH responding to dapsone. Plan: Continue dapsone 50mg. Attempt to taper to 25mg in 2 months if GFD strictly maintained. Monitor hemoglobin monthly x 3 months.

hemoglobin.flag: NORMAL â€” slight drop expected on dapsone

records.content: 41 y/o female. Celiac in remission. DH treated with dapsone. GFD strict. Hypothyroidism stable. No IDA recurrence. Weight 135 lbs. Assessment: Overall excellent. Plan: Annual labs.
41 y/o female. DH in remission x 3 months. GFD strictly maintained. Plan: Taper dapsone to 25mg daily. If remission maintained x 3 months, discontinue.
42 y/o female. DH in remission on dapsone 25mg x 3 months. No recurrence. GFD strictly maintained. Plan: Discontinue dapsone. Monitor for recurrence. GFD is the long-term treatment.

42 y/o female with celiac disease. On GFD x 3 years. tTG-IgA consistently negative. Symptoms in complete remission. Iron stores normal. Nutritional status normalized. Assessment: Celiac disease in complete remission on GFD. Plan: Annual tTG-IgA. Annual nutritional labs. Repeat DEXA at 5-year mark (2026). Continue GFD lifelong.

42 y/o female. Doing excellently. All conditions stable. Simplified medication list: levothyroxine 75mcg, vitamin D 2000 IU, calcium citrate 600mg. Assessment: Dramatic improvement from complex undiagnosed celiac patient to well-managed with simple regimen.
42 y/o female. Excellent year. Assessment: Stable. Plan: Labs.
43 y/o female. Stable. GFD x 4 years. No recurrence of IDA, DH, or GI symptoms. Assessment: Celiac disease in sustained remission.
43 y/o female. No new issues. Assessment: Stable.

43 y/o female with new bilateral hand joint stiffness and pain x 3 weeks. Morning stiffness lasting 30-40 minutes. PIP and MCP joints involved. Mild swelling bilateral. No trauma. Assessment: Inflammatory joint symptoms â€” concerning for RA (another autoimmune condition; celiac + hypothyroidism = autoimmune predisposition). Plan: Labs â€” RF, anti-CCP, ESR, CRP. If positive, rheumatology referral.

records.summary: RA labs positive â€” rheumatology referral
records.content: 43 y/o female. RF 42 (positive), anti-CCP 68 (positive â€” highly specific for RA), elevated ESR/CRP. ANA 1:80 (low positive â€” nonspecific). Assessment: Early rheumatoid arthritis, seropositive. This is the THIRD autoimmune condition (celiac + hypothyroidism + now RA). Autoimmune clustering is well-documented. Plan: Rheumatology referral. Hand X-rays.

43 y/o female with celiac disease, hypothyroidism, and new inflammatory arthritis. RF+, anti-CCP+, elevated ESR/CRP. Exam: tender and swollen PIP/MCP joints bilateral, mild grip weakness. Hand X-rays: no erosions yet â€” early disease. DAS28: 4.2 (moderate disease activity). Assessment: Seropositive RA, early, no erosions. Plan: Start methotrexate 10mg weekly + folic acid 1mg daily. LOW threshold for biologic if disease progresses (given celiac â€” must monitor GI symptoms on methotrexate). Note: Methotrexate requires monitoring â€” CBC, LFTs every 2-3 months. Also monitor celiac â€” methotrexate can affect GI mucosa.

43 y/o female. On methotrexate 10mg weekly x 5 weeks. Joint stiffness improving. Reports mild nausea on methotrexate days. No mouth sores. No GI worsening (celiac stable). Assessment: Early response to methotrexate. Plan: Continue. Labs in 2 weeks for monitoring.

43 y/o female. Complex autoimmune patient: celiac (in remission on GFD), hypothyroidism (stable on levothyroxine), early RA (responding to methotrexate). Weight 136 lbs. Vitals normal. Assessment: Three autoimmune conditions managed. RA early and responding well. Plan: Labs.

43 y/o female. RA on methotrexate 10mg weekly x 4 months. DAS28: 2.6 (low disease activity). Morning stiffness < 15 min. Mild residual PIP swelling. Assessment: RA responding well. Plan: Continue methotrexate 10mg. If not in remission by 6 months, increase dose or add biologic.
43 y/o female. All three autoimmune conditions stable. RA improving on methotrexate. Celiac in remission. Thyroid stable. Assessment: Well-managed complex autoimmune patient.

44 y/o female. RA on methotrexate 10mg weekly. DAS28: 2.2 (low disease activity, near remission). Morning stiffness resolved. Minimal joint swelling. Assessment: Near remission. Plan: Continue methotrexate. Target: DAS28 < 2.0 for clinical remission.
44 y/o female. Stable. GFD maintained. RA near remission on methotrexate. Hypothyroidism stable. Assessment: Excellent management of triple autoimmune disease.

44 y/o female. Celiac disease x 5 years on GFD. In complete remission. tTG-IgA consistently negative. No GI symptoms. Iron stores normal. DEXA to be repeated this year. Assessment: Excellent long-term celiac management. Plan: Continue GFD lifelong. Annual tTG-IgA. DEXA soon.

records.summary: HISTORICAL â€” first IDA diagnosis (3 years before current records)
records.content: HISTORICAL NOTE: Patient first diagnosed with iron deficiency anemia in 2017. Attributed to menstrual blood loss. Started on ferrous sulfate 325mg daily, later increased to BID. Iron stores never normalized on oral supplementation over 3 years. Multiple courses of oral iron tried. No GI evaluation performed at that time. Started omeprazole in 2018 for 'heartburn' (in retrospect, likely celiac-related dyspepsia).

HISTORICAL NOTE: Hypothyroidism diagnosed in 2019. TSH was 8.4, free T4 0.7. Started on levothyroxine 75mcg daily. TSH normalized. Note: Hypothyroidism is associated with celiac disease (autoimmune clustering). This association was not recognized at the time of hypothyroidism diagnosis â€” celiac screening was not performed.
38 y/o female. Hemoglobin 10.6, MCV 74 â€” still microcytic anemia. On iron BID. Assessment: Persistent IDA. Plan: Will push for IV iron trial and GI referral if not improved.
38 y/o female. Post-IV iron infusions. Feeling better. Assessment: Responding to IV iron. This suggests oral malabsorption. Plan: GI referral priority for malabsorption workup.

39 y/o female. Met with dietitian for GFD education. Learning to read food labels. Eliminating all wheat, barley, rye. Reports feeling better already after 6 weeks GFD. Assessment: Good GFD compliance. Plan: Continue education. Follow up in 3 months.
39 y/o female. Celiac on GFD x 5 months. tTG declining. Symptoms improved. Iron stores rebuilding. Assessment: Excellent early response. Plan: Continue GFD. Annual follow-up.
40 y/o female. TSH stable on levothyroxine 75mcg. No dose adjustment needed. Assessment: Hypothyroidism stable. Plan: Continue. Annual TSH.
40 y/o female. All conditions stable. Celiac in remission. IDA resolved. IBS resolved. Assessment: Doing well.
42 y/o female for annual exam. Healthy. Celiac in remission. Hypothyroidism stable. No IDA. Weight 135 lbs. Assessment: Excellent. Plan: Annual labs.

ground_truth_issues.description: Celiac disease was not diagnosed for approximately 4 years (2017-2021) despite classic presentation: refractory iron deficiency anemia unresponsive to oral supplementation, IBS-like symptoms (bloating, diarrhea), chronic fatigue, and autoimmune hypothyroidism (which should have triggered celiac screening due to well-documented association). The IDA was initially attributed to menstrual blood loss (record #69), but GYN evaluation (record #4) confirmed normal menstrual flow. Celiac serologies were not ordered until GI referral in Feb 2021 (record #16). The delay caused years of unnecessary symptoms, multiple courses of failed oral iron, need for IV iron infusions, vitamin D deficiency, osteopenia, and zinc deficiency.
ground_truth_issues.clinical_reference: ACG guidelines recommend celiac screening in: 1) Refractory iron deficiency anemia, 2) Patients with autoimmune thyroid disease, 3) Chronic diarrhea with bloating. This patient had ALL THREE indicators. Celiac disease affects ~1% of the population and is frequently missed â€” average diagnosis delay is 6-10 years.
ground_truth_issues.what_should_have_happened: Celiac screening (tTG-IgA) should have been performed at initial IDA evaluation in 2017, or at minimum when hypothyroidism was diagnosed in 2019 (autoimmune association), or when IDA proved refractory to oral iron by 2020. The combination of refractory IDA + IBS symptoms + hypothyroidism is a classic celiac triad.

ground_truth_issues.description: Omeprazole (PPI) was prescribed from 2018 onwards for 'GERD/heartburn' which was actually a manifestation of undiagnosed celiac disease. The omeprazole further impaired iron absorption (PPIs reduce gastric acid needed for non-heme iron absorption), compounding the celiac-related malabsorption and making the refractory IDA even worse. The PPI was continued for 4 years before being recognized as both unnecessary (GERD was celiac-related) and harmful (worsening iron malabsorption).
ground_truth_issues.clinical_reference: PPIs reduce gastric acid production, which is required for the reduction of ferric iron to the absorbable ferrous form. Long-term PPI use is associated with iron deficiency. In a patient with celiac-related iron malabsorption, adding a PPI creates a dual malabsorption mechanism.
ground_truth_issues.what_should_have_happened: When investigating refractory IDA in 2020, the omeprazole should have been identified as a contributing factor to iron malabsorption and stepped down or switched to an H2 blocker (which has less impact on iron absorption). The 'GERD' symptoms should have been investigated as potentially celiac-related.

ground_truth_issues.description: When hypothyroidism was diagnosed in 2019 (record #70), celiac screening was not performed despite the well-documented association between autoimmune thyroid disease and celiac disease (5-10% of celiac patients have thyroid autoimmunity, and vice versa). AGA and multiple guidelines recommend celiac screening in patients with autoimmune thyroid disease, especially when other suggestive symptoms are present (this patient had IDA and GI symptoms at the time).
ground_truth_issues.clinical_reference: AGA Technical Review on Celiac Disease (2006) and ACG Guidelines (2013) recommend celiac screening in patients with autoimmune thyroid disease. The prevalence of celiac in autoimmune thyroid patients is 2-5%, much higher than the general population (1%).
ground_truth_issues.what_should_have_happened: At the time of hypothyroidism diagnosis (2019), a tTG-IgA should have been ordered as part of autoimmune screening, especially given the concurrent refractory IDA and GI symptoms. This would have led to celiac diagnosis 2 years earlier.
